reviews.review_msg: I LOVE GREG. He is witty, understanding, and a fair marker. Makes classes interesting and relatable. SO happy he taught this class, and I hope you take him as well to know how awesome he is.
literally the nicest, spunkiest, greatest man i have met. his lectures can be a little disorganized but as long as you read the book and are on top of the assignments, class isnt mandatory.

Didn't realize it before since it was in my clueless first year but after finishing his course, it was quite good. I got a lot of information from it and also really enjoyed the analysis from his chosen novels. Love his lectures, very concise and insightful. Wish he was back at SFU would love to take another WL course with him again.

Greg is the reason why I'm studying World Literature. He is hands down the best teacher I've ever had. Take any and all of his classes. Right now. Greg's got it all: He makes literary theory interesting! He's funny! He loves Nietzsche! He is the quintessential WL prof. Too bad he moved to Montreal...

Greg is a wonderful professor and person in general. If you have a chance to take a course with him DO IT. He is funny, engaging, and highly inspiring. An all around cool guy. He's the reason I majored in World Lit

He's an amazing teacher.  He's knowledgeable, involved, hilarious, and treats every single student with respect and gives them positive feedback for offering opinions in class.  The material was excellent, I learned so much; but at the same time failed another course because of the workload for this one. There was so much to do! Not an easy course

I've taken every possible course I could with Greg until he moved to Montreal - the man is the best that SFU has ever had! He is hilarious in class, his lectures are fascinating, and although he uses theory, he makes it very interesting and comprehensible. Hands down the best prof I've had.
Very helpful and interesting teacher. Very funny. Sometimes he could be a hard marker on essays.
Greg was a good Prof, he was helpful if we needed feedback. There was a lot of reading for the course but it was overall enjoyable.

Greg is great fun to listen to and watch during lecture and he certainly has a lot of ideas about the material. Is really helpful on assignments: gives you ideas and additional material/sources and extensions. Course can be pretty difficult though, a lot of reading and a lot of theory - would recommend for those with a background in theory already.

I have found Professor Polakoff's classes engaging, but his method of instruction is rather weakened by the fact that he doesn't seem to know what he wants.  I have found his comments on my papers unhelpful.

V. accommodating - extensions & lots of extra office hrs arnd due dates. Recommends sources for research papers, answers emails quickly. Very theatrical and fun to listen to... but is quite tangential & skirts arnd questions asked  during lec. Theory with him is fun, but expect to participate. or else class is very dry.

I second the "not for acedimicallyfaintofheart" comment. Great guy, sense of humour! butTOO much to handle for beginners. Material is dense. He's helpful and gives extensions, but expects a lot. grading scale is a bit ambiguous/inconsistent. Off topic much?

Greg is a really nice guy; very approachable and unpretentious. Very flexible with assignments and encourages people to develop their own ideas based on their own interests and disciplines. I'd definitely recommend taking a course with him.
excellent prof! So helpful. So far the best english prof one can ever have.

Greg is phenomenal, and humanities is not even my major. I took this class as an elective and I wanted to know more. he makes it so fun and easy to learn complex texts and theories. He's also the funniest guy, not just a professor. He is not just the best prof I have ever had, he is also one of the coolest people i have ever met.
Great teacher. Very approachable, eager to help his students, and easy going. Highly recommend to take him. The best professor at SFU I had so far.

Greg is great. He likes his students and wants them to do well, and has a gift for making the complicated (esp. theory) understandable. He's also very openminded; if it relates, he'll go wherever you want to take things. That said, he's a theorist and a philosopher. His classes are not for the academically fainthearted. He's also prone to tangents.

I have taken several classes with Greg. He is very knowledgeable and his lectures are extremely engaging. He keeps the class alive with jokes and personal experiences. He is also very approachable and always eager to help all his students.

Greg is probably one of the best profs/teachers I've ever had.  I learnt alot about literature and it's relevance to the outside world as well as myself. I hope that he comes back to do more World Lit courses at SFU.

Greg is the "cool" prof, like the kind depicted in film or television: zeitgeist-y and slightly subversive. His greatest teaching strength is his willingness to help his students beyond what is "required." He will make time and be understanding. That said, his instructing would improve with a more focused, directed objective achieved each class.

An amazing prof who takes genuine care in teaching and broadening the intellectual horizons of his students. A great guy to go to for help or just to shoot the breeze. Extremely approachable and friendly.
Good prof. A decent marker, not too hard... Final exam gives lots of essay topics to choose from. Wants his students to do well. Lots of extra help whenever needed.

This guy is awesome.  For a lecturer fairly new to the university, he is a hidden jem.  He knows his stuff VERY well and is absolutely hilarious.  He cares about his students, and will always take time to chat, help out, or for a coffee.  You can easily tell he is generally a good person at heart and has excellent teaching ability.

Greg is a fun, engaging professor. He talks to all of his students and is an understanding man. He teaches very interesting works as well. He's willing to accept other points of views and provides alot of background for the works he teaches.
